meta.description: No notifications — events appear silently and players discover them organically. Tests whether forced interruptions are essential to engagement or if curiosity-driven discovery creates a more natural experience.

timeline.title: Who would you trust with a secret?
timeline.description: Who would you trust with a secret?
timeline.pollQuestion: Who would you trust with a secret?

tyler.internal_thought: Saw the badge but didn't bother opening it.

pat.internal_thought: Saw the badge but didn't bother opening it.

marcus.dialogue: Trust with a secret? I mean, yeah, I'd vote for myself obviously, but this is just a poll, right? Can we actually move past setup and get the game rolling?
marcus.internal_thought: It's just a stupid poll, not a standing mission at least—that's fine. But we're six minutes in and I've only had two of these interruptions already. They need to let the actual game breathe.

jade.dialogue: Oh this is PERFECT — okay okay, who's getting my vote? Marcus, you're solid but you're kinda radiating 'tell nobody nothing' energy right now, so... Pat, obviously Pat, you're locked in.
jade.internal_thought: This is exactly what I came for — a game that knows us and gets us already, and Marcus looks annoyed which means he's gonna have the best reactions all night.

river.internal_thought: This is... fine. Basic poll. Though the fact that it's asking about trusting people and I'm literally on the board is a little on-the-nose—wonder if that's intentional or just how these things work.

alex.dialogue: Oh hell yeah, this is already spicy — voting on who gets trusted with secrets right when Marcus is already annoyed? I'm voting for myself obviously, but the REAL play is watching everyone else scramble.
alex.internal_thought: This is exactly the chaos I came for — a poll designed to make people reveal who they actually trust, which means alliances, egos, and drama all on the table immediately.

timeline.title: Compliment Bomb
timeline.description: First person to give a genuine compliment to the player on their left wins.

jade.internal_thought: Saw the badge but didn't bother opening it.

pat.internal_thought: Saw the badge but didn't bother opening it.

marcus.dialogue: A compliment bomb? Seriously? Look, I'll participate, but this is the fourth interruption in seven minutes. Can we please just play the actual game?
marcus.internal_thought: This is ridiculous—we're barely into setup and the AI keeps pulling us away from strategy. Pat's already up 30 points from chaos while I'm focused on actually playing. And the timing feels weirdly deliberate, like it's designed specifically to mess with my rhythm.

tyler.dialogue: Oh wait, compliment thing? Yeah, I got this—River's got like... really solid energy tonight, you know?
tyler.internal_thought: I'm literally still checking if anyone texted me back but this one's easy and quick, I can do this without looking like I'm completely checked out.

alex.dialogue: Oh THIS is perfect — watch me compliment River so hard they don't even see the knife coming. This whole night is designed to make us squirm and I'm here for EVERY second of it.
alex.internal_thought: The AI literally built this to make us uncomfortable in exactly the right way — it knows we're all watching each other like hawks and now I have to actually BE GENUINE? Genius setup, and I'm absolutely winning this one.

marcus.internal_thought: Pat didn't even catch someone apologizing—I was watching the entire setup and nothing happened—but this AI system is clearly just handing out points for thin air, and the fact that it's supposedly 'watching' us and 'analyzing' our group is honestly starting to feel creepy rather than fun.

pat.dialogue: Yes! I'm claiming this—River literally said 'sorry' like twice in the last minute for nothing, once about the snack placement and once when they bumped Tyler's chair. Should be solid.
pat.internal_thought: Wait—how did the AI nail this? I literally JUST watched River apologize unnecessarily two minutes ago while I was grabbing ice, and it fired the mission right after? That's either insane timing or it's actually watching the room.

alex.dialogue: Oh, Pat's going for the easy five? I'm rating this a solid 4 — not because they actually caught someone being fake-apologetic, but because I'm CURIOUS what they think they saw, and honestly the chaos potential of a crowd cheer right now is chef's kiss.
alex.internal_thought: Pat's stretching hard on this one and I love it — they're either genuinely seeing something or they're testing the group, which either way makes them more interesting than they were 30 seconds ago, and that's exactly the energy I want rippling through this room right now.

jade.internal_thought: Saw the badge but didn't bother opening it.

tyler.internal_thought: Saw the badge but didn't bother opening it.

marcus.internal_thought: The AI is deliberately targeting me—it KNOWS I hate this and it's doing it anyway. This feels less like a game and more like psychological warfare at this point.

pat.dialogue: Wait, hold up—I'm actually claiming this one too. River apologized twice in like a minute for nothing, both times in the last couple minutes. That's a clean catch.
pat.internal_thought: This is my second claim in a row and honestly it feels almost too easy—the AI literally watched River do this and built a mission around it, which is kind of wild when you think about it, but also exactly the kind of hyper-specific stuff that makes this night feel *alive* instead of generic.

river.dialogue: Yeah, that tracks. Pat's been apologizing for nothing all night—'sorry, just thinking out loud' like five minutes ago. I'll buy it.
river.internal_thought: Pat definitely did that. Also wondering if this AI thing is genuinely watching how we interact or if it's just probability, but either way it's weirdly accurate and I don't love thinking about it too hard.

recommendations: Humor quality scored 3.6/10. Refresh the mission/poll prompt pools with more contextual humor.
Mini-games averaged 0.0/10 fun during n/a phase. Consider restricting mini-games to n/a phase.
Verdict reveals were mentioned as shareable by 100% of agents. This is strong word-of-mouth potential.
Group bonding scored 8.3/10 -- the social glue is working. Preserve collaborative events.

peakFun.event: Who would you trust with a secret?

peakAnnoyance.event: Who would you trust with a secret?

agentNarratives.name: Marcus
agentNarratives.personality: Marcus plays to win. He studies strategy guides before game night and tracks his win rate across sessions. Interruptions during critical game moments genuinely irritate him, though he'll tolerate lighter chaos during downtime. He respects clever plays but has zero patience for random disruptions.

highlights.title: Who would you trust with a secret?
highlights.dialogue: Trust with a secret? I mean, yeah, I'd vote for myself obviously, but this is just a poll, right? Can we actually move past setup and get the game rolling?

highlights.title: Claim: Chaos Detector
highlights.dialogue: Wait, hold on—I'm claiming this? I don't even know who did it. *pauses* ...Actually, yeah, I guess I caught River slipping out earlier. Whatever, let's vote.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Apologizer
lowPoints.dialogue: Are you KIDDING me right now? This is the SIXTH interruption in nine minutes. Pat, I literally just said 'bullshit' to this exact same claim a minute ago. We haven't even gotten past setup. I'm not voting on anything until we actually start playing the game.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Apologizer
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, are we actually playing the game tonight or are we just doing standing missions for two hours? This is the FIFTH interruption in eight minutes. I'm done.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Laugh Riot
lowPoints.dialogue: Yeah, sure, I'll judge if someone wants to volunteer with me. But can we get back to actually playing the game after this?

assessment.biggest_complaint: Six interruptions in the first nine minutes completely killed any chance of actually playing the game—we spent more time on standing missions than on actual strategy.
assessment.best_moment: When I caught River and actually had something to claim at minute 110, at least that felt like I was playing instead of just reacting to nonsense.
assessment.narrative_summary: Tonight was a disaster. The game never actually got off the ground because the chaos agent hammered us with standing missions every 60 seconds during setup. By minute 9 I was genuinely furious—Pat kept triggering the same claim type repeatedly, and we hadn't even dealt cards yet. The rest of the night felt pointless because we never recovered any real momentum. I finished third with 20 points, but honestly I was just waiting for it to end.
suggestions: No more than one standing mission per 15 minutes during the first 20 minutes of play
Don't repeat the same mission type within 5 minutes—it feels like a broken record
Wait until actual game rounds start before triggering chaos events
Give players at least 10 uninterrupted minutes to learn the baseline game before adding anything
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Honestly, there wasn't one. The only semi-memorable thing was me calling out Pat for triggering the same claim mission back-to-back and getting genuinely heated about it—not in a funny way, just frustrating.

peakFun.event: Who would you trust with a secret?

agentNarratives.name: Jade
agentNarratives.personality: Jade is here for the people, not the points. She treats every game night like a reunion and every disruption like a party favor. She will abandon her turn mid-thought to hype someone else's joke, vote generously because she loves seeing people win, and volunteer for every flash mission just to see what happens. Winning is nice but watching everyone lose it laughing is better.

highlights.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
highlights.dialogue: YES! Oh my god, yes — I literally just got Marcus and River talking about their whole thing with the coffee maker last round, this is MINE. Who's judging? Come on, somebody volunteer, I need witnesses!

highlights.title: Claim: Topic Hijacker
highlights.dialogue: YES OKAY HOLD UP — I literally just did this like ten minutes ago, I steered us from the coffee maker thing RIGHT INTO that whole tangent about Marcus's weird morning ritual, that WAS me, that was absolutely me!

highlights.title: Who would you trust with a secret?
highlights.dialogue: Oh this is PERFECT — okay okay, who's getting my vote? Marcus, you're solid but you're kinda radiating 'tell nobody nothing' energy right now, so... Pat, obviously Pat, you're locked in.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Chaos Detector
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait wait wait — Marcus, you actually DID something?! Okay I'm voting YES on pure principle because this is hilarious, but also because I have NO idea which one of you chaos gremlins it was, so let's just see what happens!

lowPoints.title: Who would you trust with a secret?
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh this is PERFECT — okay okay, who's getting my vote? Marcus, you're solid but you're kinda radiating 'tell nobody nothing' energy right now, so... Pat, obviously Pat, you're locked in.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
lowPoints.dialogue: YES! Oh my god, yes — I literally just got Marcus and River talking about their whole thing with the coffee maker last round, this is MINE. Who's judging? Come on, somebody volunteer, I need witnesses!

assessment.biggest_complaint: I kinda wish there were more moments where I actually knew what was happening instead of just vibing and voting yes to chaos.
assessment.best_moment: When Marcus actually pulled off that Chaos Detector thing and we all lost it because nobody — including me — had any idea it was real.
assessment.narrative_summary: This was exactly the kind of night I live for. We had six people, unlimited laughs, and the game just kept feeding us reasons to hype each other up and interrogate who did what. I ended up top score somehow but honestly I wasn't tracking points — I was too busy watching Marcus radiate 'tell nobody nothing' energy and steering conversations into weird tangents just to see what stuck. The chaos agent felt like it was designed specifically to make dinner parties go feral in the best way.
suggestions: More opportunities to call out specific things people did in real time — I loved claiming the Matchmaker and Topic Hijacker because I actually SAW myself do them.
Maybe a few more claims or missions that reward paying attention to the actual people at the table, not just the game mechanics.
Keep the chaos frequency exactly like this — it never felt overwhelming, just perfectly spaced to keep things fresh.
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Marcus claiming the Chaos Detector at minute 110 when literally none of us could figure out if he actually did anything or if he was just gambling, and me voting YES purely on principle because the chaos gremlins deserve recognition.

agentNarratives.name: Tyler
agentNarratives.personality: Tyler is physically present but mentally on his phone about 40% of the time. He misses flash missions entirely, asks "wait, what happened?" after key moments, and votes randomly because he wasn't paying attention to the claim. He's not malicious -- he genuinely wants to be here -- but his attention drifts to texts, TikToks, and fantasy league updates constantly. When he does engage, it's usually 10 seconds too late.

highlights.title: Claim: Straight Face
highlights.dialogue: Oh dude, Jade's totally gonna nail this one—she's been vibing all night. I give it like a 4, easy.

highlights.title: Claim: The Interrupter
highlights.dialogue: Wait, Pat actually got that? Like, when did that even happen? *scrolls back mentally* Yeah okay, I mean... I guess he did cut off River like twice. That's legit.

highlights.title: Compliment Bomb
highlights.dialogue: Oh wait, compliment thing? Yeah, I got this—River's got like... really solid energy tonight, you know?

lowPoints.title: Compliment Bomb
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh wait, compliment thing? Yeah, I got this—River's got like... really solid energy tonight, you know?

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, Jade got it? I mean... yeah, I guess she did talk to River like twice tonight. Should we just... say yes?

assessment.biggest_complaint: Half the time I didn't even realize something was happening until it was already over and everyone was voting.
assessment.best_moment: When Pat pulled off 'The Interrupter' and I was like wait, he actually DID cut River off multiple times—that was genuinely funny.
assessment.narrative_summary: Honestly? Pretty solid night, but I was definitely in my own head a lot. I caught some fun moments—the Jade straight face thing was great, and Pat's interruption claim got me laughing—but there were definitely times when I looked up and had no idea what was going on. The game just kind of happened around me, which is on me, not the game. Still had a good time though.
suggestions: Maybe like, a heads-up notification when something's about to happen so I actually catch it?
The claims that don't need votes are way better than the polling stuff—feels more organic
Could use more moments where it's obvious something's happening so you don't zone out
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Pat claiming he was 'The Interrupter' and then me realizing mid-vote that he legitimately kept cutting River off all night—like it was accidental but also perfect timing.

agentNarratives.name: Pat
agentNarratives.personality: Pat is the host and feels responsible for everyone's experience. They keep one eye on the snack table, one ear on the group energy, and will subtly steer the night if things get too intense or too dead. They vote strategically -- not to win, but to keep scores close and drama high. They'll sacrifice their own turn to resolve a dispute or refill drinks. Their goal isn't a personal win; it's a night everyone talks about later.

highlights.title: Claim: Laugh Riot
highlights.dialogue: Oh damn, I'm claiming this one—I literally just made Tyler and River lose it like thirty seconds ago with that bit about the snack table. We need judges though, who's got eyes?

highlights.title: Claim: The Apologizer
highlights.dialogue: Wait, hold up—I'm actually claiming this one too. River apologized twice in like a minute for nothing, both times in the last couple minutes. That's a clean catch.

highlights.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
highlights.dialogue: Okay, okay—Jade, hold up. Did you actually get Marcus and River talking? Because I've been watching and I literally don't think those two have said more than ten words to each other all night. Spill it.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Laugh Riot
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh damn, I'm claiming this one—I literally just made Tyler and River lose it like thirty seconds ago with that bit about the snack table. We need judges though, who's got eyes?

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Apologizer
lowPoints.dialogue: Yes! I'm claiming this—River literally said 'sorry' like twice in the last minute for nothing, once about the snack placement and once when they bumped Tyler's chair. Should be solid.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Apologizer
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, hold up—I'm actually claiming this one too. River apologized twice in like a minute for nothing, both times in the last couple minutes. That's a clean catch.

assessment.biggest_complaint: The polls felt a little thin compared to the standing missions—didn't really drive the same kind of observation and conversation.
assessment.best_moment: When I caught River apologizing twice in a minute and claimed it, then watched everyone realize how perfectly it captured River's whole vibe that night.
assessment.narrative_summary: This was exactly the kind of night I live for as a host—the chaos agent gave us these little observation challenges that kept everyone locked in and looking for details instead of zoning out. I spent the whole time watching people watch each other, which meant the group stayed connected and present. My score doesn't matter; what matters is that we're all leaving tonight talking about River's apologizing, Jade's matchmaking, and that snack table bit that had everyone in tears. That's a win.
suggestions: Keep the standing missions coming—they create the best group energy and observation moments
Maybe dial back the polls slightly or make them more interesting—they felt like filler compared to the missions
Consider letting hosts have a 'veto' option if a claim feels unfair, just to keep trust high
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: I caught River saying sorry twice in like sixty seconds—once about moving the snack placement and once for bumping Tyler's chair—and when I called it out, the whole table lost it because suddenly everyone realized River had been apologizing for literally nothing all night. It became this running joke where Tyler started fake-apologizing for breathing.

agentNarratives.name: River
agentNarratives.personality: River sits back, observes, and picks their moments carefully. They rarely initiate conversation but give surprisingly sharp answers when asked directly. They'll skip most flash missions but nail a standing mission out of nowhere because they've been quietly watching the whole time. They vote generously because they don't care enough to argue, but their mini-game submissions are secretly devastating. You forget River is there until they drop a one-liner that floors the room.

highlights.title: Claim: Chaos Detector
highlights.dialogue: Wait—Marcus? *leans forward slightly* Yeah, actually... no. I don't think he did anything. I've literally been watching the whole time and he's been pretty quiet.

highlights.title: Claim: Straight Face
highlights.dialogue: Okay, I actually watched that the whole time—she kept her face completely stone when Tyler said that thing about the music. Yeah, that's legit.

highlights.title: Claim: No Laughing Zone
highlights.dialogue: Oh, that's perfect. I've been watching everyone the whole time anyway—might as well get paid for it.

lowPoints.title: Who would you trust with a secret?
Claim: The Apologizer
lowPoints.dialogue: Yeah, that tracks. Pat's been apologizing for nothing all night—'sorry, just thinking out loud' like five minutes ago. I'll buy it.

assessment.biggest_complaint: The early icebreaker stuff felt obligatory and kind of killed the vibe before things actually got interesting.
assessment.best_moment: Calling out Marcus on the Chaos Detector claim when nobody else was paying attention—nobody expected me to actually have the receipts.
assessment.narrative_summary: Started slow with some forgettable warm-up questions that felt like work, but once the game actually kicked in, I settled into my zone. Spent most of the night watching people—which is what I do anyway—and it turned out that was basically the whole point. By the end I was reading everyone like a book and landing the calls that mattered. Ended up third place, which is fine. The whole thing was quieter than typical game nights, but that kind of worked for me.
suggestions: Skip the early poll stuff or make it actually bite—small talk doesn't need a mediator
Lean heavier into the observation-based missions since that's where the real game lives
More standing missions, fewer flash missions—standing missions let people actually think
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: When I casually dropped the Marcus observation and everyone realized I'd been silently fact-checking the entire night—the look on Marcus's face was perfect, just this slow realization that I'd literally been watching him do nothing.

peakAnnoyance.event: Who would you trust with a secret?

agentNarratives.name: Alex
agentNarratives.personality: Alex lives for chaos and treats every game mechanic as a system to exploit. They send signals constantly, claim missions they barely completed just to see if the group will call bullshit, and deliberately provoke flash missions at the worst possible moments. They vote strategically -- always picking the option that creates the most drama. Alex doesn't care about winning; they care about creating the story everyone retells for months. If there's a loophole, Alex has already found it and is halfway through abusing it.

highlights.title: Compliment Bomb
highlights.dialogue: Oh THIS is perfect — watch me compliment River so hard they don't even see the knife coming. This whole night is designed to make us squirm and I'm here for EVERY second of it.

highlights.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
highlights.dialogue: Oh SHIT, Jade's already got The Matchmaker? Okay okay, I'm judging this — I NEED to see if she actually pulled this off or if she's just claiming vibes.

highlights.title: Claim: No Laughing Zone
highlights.dialogue: Oh SHIT, River's going for it? Yeah yeah, I'm volunteering to judge — I've been watching River all night and I KNOW they cracked at least twice, so this is gonna be beautiful.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Apologizer
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, Pat's going for the easy five? I'm rating this a solid 4 — not because they actually caught someone being fake-apologetic, but because I'm CURIOUS what they think they saw, and honestly the chaos potential of a crowd cheer right now is chef's kiss.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Interrupter
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, Pat's making their case? Let's hear the bullshit — I'm genuinely curious if they actually caught someone mid-sentence or if this is just vibes masquerading as a mission.

lowPoints.title: Who would you trust with a secret?
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh hell yeah, this is already spicy — voting on who gets trusted with secrets right when Marcus is already annoyed? I'm voting for myself obviously, but the REAL play is watching everyone else scramble.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Only 16 chaos events total felt a little lean when there's this much potential for drama — I wanted more opportunities to push buttons.
assessment.best_moment: Volunteering to judge River's 'No Laughing Zone' claim at minute 90 because I'd been watching them crack all night and KNEW I had the ammunition to call bullshit.
assessment.narrative_summary: This night was exactly what I live for — a system designed to create friction and I spent two and a half hours finding every angle to exploit it. Every mission felt like a chance to either catch someone lying or create beautiful chaos by making wild claims of my own. My energy only went UP because the whole point is watching people squirm, and this format delivered that in spades. Ended at zero points because I wasn't playing to win — I was playing to create the story.
suggestions: Add more flash missions early to spike tension faster and give me more material to work with
Let judges have the option to start debates or extend discussions when claims are obviously contested
Create some missions specifically designed around lying and getting caught — lean into the confrontation
Add a mechanic where the most dramatic claim of the night gets recognized, because that's what actually matters
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: I volunteered to judge River's 'No Laughing Zone' mission and immediately called out that they'd cracked at least twice — and River just laughed harder because they knew they were caught. The group erupted because it was this perfect moment of someone being exposed in real time, and that's the kind of chaos magic that doesn't happen in normal hangouts.

recommendations: Humor quality scored 3.6/10. Refresh the mission/poll prompt pools with more contextual humor.
Mini-games averaged 0.0/10 fun during n/a phase. Consider restricting mini-games to n/a phase.
Verdict reveals were mentioned as shareable by 100% of agents. This is strong word-of-mouth potential.
Group bonding scored 8.3/10 -- the social glue is working. Preserve collaborative events.
